title: The -li diminutive
text:
Swiss German loves making things small and friendly by adding -li:

• Kafi → Käfeli (a little coffee)
• Stund → Stündli (a little hour)
• Hund → Hündli (a little dog)
• Sach → Sächeli (a little thing)

The vowel often shifts (a→ä, u→ü, o→ö) when -li is added.

It's not always literally small — 'mir göhnd schnäll es Bierli go näh' might mean three beers. The -li makes things sound cosy and informal, which matters a lot here.

title: Little words: gäll, halt, eba, scho, äuä
text:
These tiny words carry the tone of a sentence. Master them and you sound local:

• gäll — 'right?, isn't it?' tacked on the end seeking agreement: "Schöns Wätter, gäll?"
• halt — 'just/simply', a resigned shrug: "Es isch halt so."
• eba — 'exactly', agreeing someone's right.
• scho — 'admittedly/sure...but': "Es isch scho guet, aber..."
• äuä — doubt: 'probably not / hardly'.

You can't learn these from a textbook — listen, then try them.

title: Past tense with ha and bi
text:
Swiss German has no simple past — everything past uses 'ha' (have) or 'bi' (be) + past participle:

• Ich ha es Znacht ghaa. (I had dinner.)
• Mir sind go wandere gange. (We went hiking.)
• Ich ha en Fründ troffe. (I met a friend.)

Motion/change verbs take 'bi/sind' (bi gange, bi cho); most others take 'ha/händ'.

The participle usually lands at the end of the clause.

title: Everyday Swiss words
text:
Some words are simply different here — often borrowed from French:

• Velo = bicycle (not Fahrrad)
• Natel = mobile phone
• Glace = ice cream
• Merci = thanks
• Stutz = francs, Rappe = cents
• Znüni / Zvieri = mid-morning / afternoon snack
• Finke = slippers
• poschte = to shop / run errands

Using these makes you sound instantly more at home.

title: ich/i, isch, nöd, cha
text:
Core sound shifts that mark the dialect:

• ist → isch (is); the German 'st' becomes 'sch'.
• nicht → nöd (not)
• kann → cha, kannst → chasch (can) — German 'k' often becomes 'ch'.
• kommen → cho/chume, kommst → chunsch
• sehen → gseh (to see)
• etwas → öppis (something), jemand → öpper (someone)

Good (guet), nice (schöö) and small (chli) round out the everyday basics.

title: Telling time: halbi, ab, vor
text:
Time works like German but watch 'halbi':

• halbi achti = half TO eight = 7:30 (not 8:30!)
• Viertel ab achti = quarter past eight = 8:15
• Viertel vor nüni = quarter to nine = 8:45

And punctuality is taken seriously — 'am Viertel ab zwöi' means exactly that, not a few minutes later.

title: Grüezi vs Sali — formal and familiar
text:
Choose your greeting by relationship:

• Grüezi — polite, for people you don't know (and Sie with it)
• Sali / Hoi — casual, for friends (and du)

Switching from Sie to du is a small milestone of trust — often someone offers: 'Säge mer doch du?' Using du too early can seem rude; staying on Sie too long can seem stiff.
